segments.s151-g129: ز شب نیمه‌ای = a time phrase: ز (a poetic preposition 'from/at') + شب نیمه‌ای ('a half of the night' with the adjectival/-ای ending that makes it 'a middle/one half'). گفت is the simple past 'spoke/said'. سهراب بود places the proper name after the verb with the copula بود ('was'); classical/epic word order often follows Verb + Subject + بود to mean 'it was Sohrab who spoke' or 'Sohrab spoke' (past narrative).
segments.s151-g130: دگر نیمه = 'the other/remaining half' (دگر as determiner 'other' before نیمه). آرامش و خواب forms a compound predicate 'tranquility and sleep' (two nouns joined by و). بود is the past copula 'was' concluding the clause: 'the other half was tranquility and sleep' — straightforward subject (دگر نیمه) + predicate structure, typical poetic compression.
segments.s151-g25: کنون (adverb) = 'now'; خوان (noun) = 'feast/table/banquet' is the direct object of the implied action; همی is an archaic emphatic/continuous particle often found in epic Persian and here strengthens the modal; باید is an impersonal modal 'must/ought to'; آراستن is the infinitive 'to adorn/prepare' that follows باید (the auxiliary precedes the infinitive in this poetic word order).
segments.s151-g26: بباید is a variant/repetition of the modal 'it is necessary' (intensifying the necessity); به + می (here compresses to می‌غم in the token list) marks the means 'with/by wine'; غم ز دل کاستن is the verbal phrase 'to remove/lessen sorrow from the heart' where کاستن is the infinitive 'to lessen/take away' and غم ز دل is its object (ز = 'from', دل = 'heart'). Note the infinitive is placed at the end, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s151-g27: وزان (poetic compound of و + از + آن) functions adverbially: 'from that / at that (sight)'; روی here means 'appearance/sight' (literally 'face'); رستم is the subject; سپه را is the definite object with the postpositional object marker را; بدید is simple past 'saw'. Word order is subject after the adverbial phrase, with را marking the object as expected.
segments.s151-g28: سخن راند is a poetic phrasing 'he uttered speech / he spoke' (سخن = speech; راند here functions as the verb 'uttered/spoke' in older epic usage); با گیو = 'to/with Giv' (با marks the interlocutor); و links clauses; گفت و شنید is a paired phrase meaning 'there was talking and hearing' or 'they spoke and listened' — گفت (said/spoke) and شنید (heard) are complementary actions describing the dialogue.
segments.s151-g29: که is a subordinating particle 'that' introducing reported content; امروز 'today' is temporal adverb; سهراب is the proper name subject; رزم‌آزمای is a compound epithet (رزم = 'battle', آزمای from آَزما = 'testing') meaning 'one who tests in battle' or roughly 'battle‑prover / formidable warrior' and functions as a post‑nominal epithet describing سهراب.
segments.s151-g30: چگونه 'how' introduces the manner; به جنگ اندر آورد پای is an idiomatic poetic sequence: به جنگ 'into the battle', اندر آورد (آوردن in a causative/past sense) 'brought/caused to enter' and پای 'foot' used idiomatically ('to put/set foot'). Together they mean 'how he set foot/entered into the fight.' Note the infinitive/nominal element پای follows the verb phrase, as is common in older word order.
segments.s151-g31: چنین گفت = 'thus said'; با رستم indicates the addressee 'to/with Rostam' (با here functions to mark the person to whom speech is directed); گرد گیو places the name گیو with گرد — گرد in epic usage can be an epithet or an adverbial/relational element attached to the name; syntactically the clause identifies Giv as the speaker and marks Rostam as the interlocutor.
segments.s151-g32: کزین = 'from this / of this sort'; گونه = 'kind, manner' (here 'such a kind'); هرگز = 'never'; ندیدیم = 'we have not seen' (past indicative, first person plural); نیو is an older/regional form meaning 'new'/'the like' (i.e. 'such as this' or 'its equal'), so the full sense is 'we have never seen one like this.'
segments.s151-g105: مباشید is a negative imperative/optative in the plural (literary), 'do not be / let there not be'. یک تن literally 'one body' functions here as 'a single person'. بر + ین is written as two pieces: بر 'on/upon' + ین (here = این) 'this', so برین رزمگاه = 'on/at this battlefield' (rhematic noun follows the locative particle). Word order: negative imperative → quantified noun → locative phrase.
segments.s151-g106: مسازید is negative imperative/plural of ساختن/سازیدن 'do not make/produce/bring about'. جستن is the infinitive 'to seek/attempt' used after مسازید to indicate the action not to be done. سوی رزم راه is a noun phrase 'toward the road/path of battle' (سوی = toward; رزم = battle; راه = road/path). The verb phrase governs the following infinitive phrase, so: 'do not cause/let (them) seek the road toward battle.'
segments.s151-g107: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each and every'. سوی marks direction 'toward'. زابلستان is a place-name (Zabulistan). شوید is the present/subjunctive/imperative-form of شدن used here as an exhortation 'go / become / proceed' in literary register (second-person plural/formal). Word order: adverb → direction → place → verbal exhortation.
segments.s151-g108: از ایدر 'from here' (از = from; ایدر = here). به نزدیک = 'to/toward the vicinity/near (of)'; به is a directional preposition, نزدیک is 'near/nearby'. دستان is a toponym/personal/place reference in context (here rendered as 'Dastan'/'the Dastan area'); شوید repeats the exhortative/optative use of شدن 'proceed / go'. Entire phrase gives a second directional order: 'from here, go toward the nearby Dastan.'
segments.s151-g109: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. خرسند is an adjective 'pleased / glad'. گردان here functions as an imperative/causative verbal form (from گرداندن) 'make' — in this segment it governs دل مادرم 'my mother's heart' (دل = heart; مادر + م = mother + my). Word order: vocative/subject → adjective (stative or result) → verb (causative) → object (possessed noun). Note: the edition splits the possessive -م as a separate token; that -م is a possessive suffix ('my'), not the copula.
segments.s151-g110: چنین 'thus/so' is an adverb framing what follows. کرد is the past tense of کردن 'did / made / caused' (here used intransitively as 'was done'). یزدان قضا is a nominative noun phrase 'God-fate' (divine fate). بر is the preposition 'upon/on', and سرم is 'my head' (here idiomatic for 'to me / upon me'); together بر سرم = 'upon me / to me'. The clause states that this is what divine fate has effected upon the speaker.
segments.s151-g111: بگویش is the imperative 'tell him/her' (بگو + 3rd-person object enclitic -ش). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. تو دل به من در مبند is an instruction: تو = you; دل = heart; به من = to me; در (here) = 'in/to' or marker used with مبند; مبند is negative imperative of بستن 'do not bind'. The verb (مبند) comes last (typical Persian SOV ordering) and negation is formed by the negative imperative form.
segments.s151-g112: که again introduces explanation 'for/because'. سودی 'benefit/profit' is the object; ندارت is the familiar literary 2nd/3rd-person negative present of داشتن 'you have not / there is not for you'. بودن is the infinitive 'to be' (here part of a nominal comment), and نژند is an adjective meaning 'base / worthless'. Together: 'for you will have no benefit; to be so is base/worthless.'
segments.s151-g41: نتابید is the past negative of تابیدن used here in the sense 'to endure/stand' (they could not endure). با او is a prepositional phrase 'with him' placed after the verb for emphasis. بتابید is another past-tense form of تابیدن used in the sense 'to turn' (they turned), and روی is the noun 'face' (their faces); together بتابید روی means 'they turned their faces'. The sequence verb + prepositional phrase + verb + object is typical of older/poetic word order where repeated verbal forms create contrastive actions.
segments.s151-g42: شدند is past plural 'became' (they became). از introduces the group 'of/from' which selects دلیران 'the brave' as the source/people. بسی here functions adverbially ('many/enough') modifying جنگ جوی, a compound where جنگ is 'war/battle' and جوی ('-jūy') is a deverbal/agentive element meaning 'seeker' or 'one who searches for'. The whole phrase شدند از دلیران بسی جنگ جوی = 'many of the brave became seekers of battle.'
segments.s151-g43: ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from/among'. گردان = 'troop/company' marks the group being spoken about. کسی here, used with the negative verb, means 'no one' (not 'someone'); مایه is 'resource/support', followed by the possessor او 'for him'. نداشت is the past-tense negative of داشتن: 'did not have.' Word order is 'from the troop no one resource him had' — typical subject/object/verb order in Persian with negation on the verb.
segments.s151-g44: جز 'except' introduces the sole exception. از is 'from', پیلتن is a place-name (Piltan) serving as the source of support, and پایه here means 'foundation/support'. او نداشت repeats the negative past 'he had not' — so the clause says he had no support except that from Piltan. Note the repetition of نداشت mirrors the first clause to stress the absence of backing.
segments.s151-g45: هم is an adverb 'also/likewise' fronting the clause. آیین پیشین is a noun phrase 'the former/customary ritual' (آیین = custom/practice, پیشین = former). نگه داشتیم is the compound verb 'to keep/maintain' in the first person plural past 'we kept/maintained' (نگه + داشتیم). The order places the adverb and object before the verb, common in narrative Persian.
segments.s151-g46: سپاهی is the noun 'soldiers/army'. برو here functions as a modifier indicating the type/role of the soldiers (classically used to mark 'going/foot' or simply 'those who go'), and ساده 'simple/ordinary' further qualifies them. بگماشتیم is past first plural 'we placed/appointed/stationed.' Grammatically this is a noun phrase (سپاهی برو ساده) followed by the verb 'we stationed.'
segments.s151-g47: سواری 'horseman/rider' is the subject. نشد is past negative of شدن used here in the sense 'did not happen/there was not' (i.e., 'there was not a rider'). پیش او means 'before him/in his presence.' یکتنه 'one, single' emphasizes that there was not even one rider. The clause literally reads 'a horseman was not before him, one whole' = 'not a single horseman stood before him.'
segments.s151-g48: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/still' and modifies the verb تاخت 'galloped/charged' (past). از ... تا marks a span 'from ... to ...' with قلب here meaning 'the heart/center' (center of the battle/line) and میمنه 'the right side.' So همی تاخت از قلب تا میمنه = 'he kept charging from the center to the right.' The verb is simple past describing a repeated/continuous action.
segments.s151-g33: بیامد is a simple past verb 'came' (he came). دمان here functions as a proper name (not one of the given noun senses) — the phrase means 'Doman came'. تا ... سپاه marks extent: تا 'up to/until', به 'to', قلب 'core/heart', سپاه 'the host/army' — word order is verb + subject/name + adverbial phrase indicating how far he advanced.
segments.s151-g34: This clause describes the reaction that followed: ز لشکر 'from the ranks/among the army' (poetic use of ز = from), بر طوس 'against Tūs' (بر = against/toward), شد کینه‌خواه is a predicative sequence: شد 'became' + کینه 'grudge' + خواه 'desiring' = 'became grudge‑seeking' (i.e. a grudge arose directed at Tūs).
segments.s151-g35: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/that'. او بود is past 'he was'; بر زین 'on the saddle' uses preposition بر + noun; و connects to نیزه بدست, where نیزه 'spear' + بدست 'in (the) hand' is an adjectival phrase 'with spear in hand'. The whole segment gives background circumstance.
segments.s151-g36: چو = 'when'; گرگین is the proper name Gorgin; فرود آمد is the compound verb 'alighted/dismounted' (فرود + آمد); او برنشست is a past action 'he sat up/on' (برنشست = sat upon or took a seat). The temporal clause links Gorgin's alighting to the subsequent seating.
segments.s151-g37: بیامد is again past 'he came/approached'. چو here = 'when'; با نیزه 'with a spear' (با = with); او را is the definite object (he/him + را); بدید is past 'saw'. The clause reads 'he came; when he saw him with a spear...'.
segments.s151-g38: به کردار means 'in the manner/character of' (به used adverbially 'by/with' + کردار 'conduct/way'); شیر ژیان is a lion‑like epithet ('شیر' = lion, ژیان here indicating the manner/way); بردمید is a perfective verb 'seized/snatched up'. Together: 'he, in lion‑like conduct, seized (him)'.
segments.s151-g39: This is a terse poetic description of the blow: عمودی 'vertical' and خمیده 'bent/curved' are stacked adjectives modifying the implied strike; بزد is the past 'he struck'; بر (on/upon) + برش (here 'at him / at his part' — literally 'on his slice/section' but contextually 'against him') marks the target. Word order places manner adjectives before the verb, then the prepositional target.
segments.s151-g40: ز نیرو 'by/from force' (ز = from/by, نیرو = force/strength) + بیفتاد 'fell' gives the causal phrase 'by force fell'. ترگ 'a rag/shred' is the thing that fell, followed by از سرش 'from his head' (از = from, سر = head, ش = his). The sequence states the result of the blow: a shred fell from his head.
segments.s151-g97: بیاور is an imperative verb 'bring!'. The two objects سپاه و درفش are coordinated by و; the possessive/accusative clitic appears on the final noun as م + را (here tokenized as م and را) and applies to the coordinated phrase, making the whole phrase the direct object of بیاور.
segments.s151-g98: همان is a demonstrative 'the same/that very' modifying تخت 'throne'. زرینه is an adjective 'golden/gilded' modifying کفش 'shoe(s)'. The possessive/accusative clitic again attaches to the final noun (م + را) and makes the whole noun phrase the object; word order is demonstrative + noun, then conjunction + adjective + noun.
segments.s151-g99: همی is an adverb meaning 'always/continually'; باش is the imperative 'be/remain'. بر پیش introduces a locative phrase 'before/in front of' and پرده‌سرای is the place-name 'curtain-hall' (a compound meaning something like stage or inner hall). The order is adverb + imperative + locative phrase.
segments.s151-g100: چو introduces a temporal/simile clause 'when/as'. خورشید تابان is a noun + adjective 'the shining sun'. برآید is third-person singular present/subjunctive of برآمدن 'rise/appear' used here as a general/habitual present 'rises'. ز جای is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from its place'.
segments.s151-g101: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدون here functions idiomatically 'it happens/comes to pass that'. که introduces the subordinate clause. پیروز is predicate adjective 'victorious'; باشم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I be / I am' used in the protasis ('if I am victorious'). به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'in battle'.
segments.s151-g102: به آوردگه is a locative phrase 'at/to the battlefield/camp'. بر is a local/prepositional particle here reinforcing location 'on/at'. نسازم is the verb 'I do not make' (ن- negative + سازم) and takes درنگ 'delay' as its object, so 'I will not make delay' = 'I will not delay'. Word order: locative phrase + particle + verb + object.
segments.s151-g103: وگر is another conditional 'and if'. خود is emphatic 'itself' or 'itself/indeed' pointing to the subject. دگرگونه 'different/otherwise' modifies the implied subject; گردد is third-person singular present/subjunctive of گردیدن 'becomes/turns out'. سخن 'speech/matter' is the noun (object/subject) naming what is changing. The clause means 'if the matter itself becomes different'.
segments.s151-g104: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'. زاری میاغاز is an imperative phrase 'begin lamenting/pleading' (می- progressive prefix + آغاذ/اغاز 'begin'); و joins clauses; تندی مکن is a prohibitive 'do not (be) harsh/impetuous' where مکن is the negative imperative 'do not'.
segments.s151-g1: برفتند is the simple past 3rd-person plural (they went/departed). The conjunction و links the two clauses. روی هوا is a genitive-like noun phrase 'the face (روی) of the air/sky (هوا)' with the possessed before the possessor (poetic inversion), and تیره گشت is a predicative phrase where گشت 'became/turned' follows the adjective تیره 'dark' (adjective + verb 'become').
segments.s151-g2: ز here functions as the causal 'because of / on account of'; it introduces the cause ('because of Sohrab'). گردون is a poetic word for 'the heavens/sky' and is the grammatical subject that follows the causal phrase; همی is an archaic continuative/emphatic particle marking a sustained or habitual sense of the verb, and خیره گشت is the compound predicate ('became/was made fixed/staring' — خیره as adjective/participle + گشت 'became').
segments.s151-g3: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past ('you said'); the provided lemma senses do not include 'to say' so the sense index for گفتی is marked -1. ز جنگش introduces an agental/causal phrase 'from/because of his fighting' (ز = 'from/because of' + جنگ + possessive -ش 'his'). سرشت is here best read as the past/participial idea of 'was fashioned/created' (poetic usage of سرشتن), and آسمان is the direct object/subject of that creation (word order is poetic, placing the verb-like سرشت before its complement).
segments.s151-g4: نیارامد is a negative past-form meaning 'did not become calm / could not be pacified' (compound negative formation with ن-). از introduces the cause 'because of / from' here and takes the verbal noun تاختن 'charging, to charge' as its object; یک زمان is a temporal phrase 'a single moment' (یک modifies زمان). The clause thus says the sky did not cease/quiet even for one moment because of his charging.
segments.s151-g5: وگر is the conditional 'if' (archaic/poetic), باره is an adverbial 'on occasion / at a time' placed after the particle. زیر اندرش is a prepositional phrase 'under / beneath in him' where اندر 'in' combines with the possessive suffix ش 'his' (poetic order: preposition + internal + possessive). آهنست is a predicate composed of آهن 'iron' plus the copular suffix -ست 'is' (poetic fused form), literally 'is iron'.
segments.s151-g6: This clause uses verbless/nominal predication common in Persian poetry: شگفتی is a nominative/predicative noun 'a marvel / wondrous thing' and روانست is an archaic fused form where روان 'soul/spirit' combines with the copula -ست ('is'); given the provided sense list does not supply the noun-copula reading, its sense index is set to -1. The conjunction و links to رویین تنست, where رویین 'iron-hard/armored' modifies تنست (تن + ست 'body is'), yielding 'and his body is iron-clad.'
segments.s151-g7: شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'dark night' with the adjective before or after interchangeable in poetry; آمد is the past 'came', and سوی لشکرش is the direction phrase 'toward his army' (سوی = toward; لشکرش = 'his army' with possessive suffix). Word order is Subject (شب تیره) + verb (آمد) + directional phrase (سوی لشکرش).
segments.s151-g8: میان functions as 'in the midst / among' (poetic placement before what follows). The word سوده is ambiguous in this context (could be a participial/abstract noun or a proper name); because the provided sense-list is unclear for this line its sense is marked -1. The two prepositional phrases از جنگ و از خنجرش ('from/because of the war and from his dagger') use از in the basic 'from / out of' sense and attach concrete nouns (جنگ 'battle', خنجرش 'his dagger' with possessive suffix).
segments.s151-g121: اگر introduces a conditional ('if/even if'). سال گشتی is an archaic idiom meaning 'you become years' = 'you live/your years amount to'; گشتی is from گشتن used impersonally for living/age, here conditional. فزون ازهزار ('more than a thousand') is a postpositive quantity phrase modifying سال (فزون = 'more/increasing', ازهزار = 'than a thousand').
segments.s151-g122: همین is the demonstrative 'this/this very'; بود + خواهد form a future copula (poetically inverted here as بود خواهد instead of خواهد بود) so the clause reads 'this will be'. سرانجام کار is the noun phrase 'the outcome/end of the affair' that the copula applies to.
segments.s151-g123: چو here = 'when'. خرسند گردد is a predicate ('becomes pleased/content'); گردد is the present/subjunctive of گشتن used as 'become'. به دستان بگوی is an instruction (imperative/infinitival sense): به + دستان ('to the hands' = to those at hand, i.e. attendants/people present) + بگوی (tell — imperative form of گفتن implied). The clause tells the addressee to inform those present when he is pleased.
segments.s151-g124: که introduces the content of what to tell them ('that ...'). از شاه گیتی is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the king of the world' (شاه گیتی = 'king of the world'). مبرتاب is a negative verbal form (poetic) meaning 'do not turn away / do not refuse / do not repulse'; روی is 'face' (literally 'turn your face away'). Together: 'that you/they do not turn their face away from the king of the world.'
segments.s151-g125: اگر introduces a conditional. جنگ سازد is a potential/conditional clause ('if he makes/wages war') where سازد is the present/subjunctive of ساختن used as 'to make/bring about'. تو سستی مکن is a direct admonition: تو (you) + سستی (laxity/weakness) + مکن (negative imperative 'do not do') = 'do not be lax.'
segments.s151-g126: چنان رو is an imperative/advice phrase 'proceed/act thus' where رو is the (poetic) imperative of رفتن 'go/advance/act'. که introduces the result/goal clause. او راند is a finite verb 'he/it drove/expelled' (راند = 'drove'); از بن سخن is an adverbial phrase 'from the root of speech' meaning 'utterly, to the very root (i.e. silencing or utterly routing him)'. So: 'Act so that he is driven from the very root of speech.'
segments.s151-g127: همه is the universal 'all/everyone'. مرگ راییم is an idiomatic, somewhat archaic phrasing meaning 'we belong to/deal with death' or 'death is our lot' — راییم here reflects an older/cliticized form involving را + the verb for 'be' to indicate that death is what we are subject to. پیر و جوان ('old and young') is a coordinated noun phrase specifying who is included.
segments.s151-g128: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world'. نماند is the negative present/past of ماندن ('does not remain / is not left'), functioning here as a general truth: 'there does not remain'. کسی in negative contexts = 'anyone/no one' (here 'no one'). جاودان is the adjective 'eternal'. Word order places the predicate before the logical subject (کسی جاودان): 'in the world no one remains eternal.'
segments.s151-g81: کزویست is a fused classical formula meaning “that is from him / belongs to him” (literally: 'it is from him'). پیروزی و دستگاه pairs two nouns (victory + dominion/court) linked by و (“and”), so the whole phrase asserts possession: “victory and dominion are his.”
segments.s151-g82: به فرمان او تابد is a prepositional phrase + verb: به here functions instrumentally as “by/at (the) command,” فرمان او = “his command,” and تابد (present/future poetic form of تافتن) means “shines.” از چرخ ماه is a locative/source phrase: از = “from,” چرخ = “the heavens/sky,” ماه = “the moon.” Together: “at his command the moon shines from the heavens.”
segments.s151-g83: کند is the simple verb “does/makes” used here with a future sense (“he will”/“he makes anew”). تازه این بار modifies کام ترا: تازه = “new,” این بار = “this time,” کام = “desire/wish,” and ت (enclitic) + را marks the second-person object (“your desire”). Word order is verb first (poetic) then the nominal phrase being affected.
segments.s151-g84: برآرد (برآوردن) here means “bring forth / raise / make appear.” به خورشید نام ترا is a purposive phrase: به = “to/into,” خورشید = “the sun,” نام = “name/fame” and ترا (ت + را) = “your (name)” with the object marker. Literally: “will lift/raise your name to the sun” (i.e. exalt your fame).
segments.s151-g85: بدو = “to him / unto him” (prepositional clitic). گفت is the past reporting verb “said” (here introducing direct speech or a subordinate clause); که introduces the content of what was said. با فر شاه = “with/by the king’s far(r)” where فر is the special Shahnameh term for royal/divine glory and شاه = king.
segments.s151-g86: برآید here means “will come to pass / be realized” (become possible). همه = “all/every,” کامه = plural/collective of کام “desires,” and نیک‌خواه is “well-wisher” (one who wishes well). The clause states that, through the king’s glory, all the desires of a well-wisher will be fulfilled.
segments.s151-g87: به لشکر گه خویش بنهاد روی: به = “toward/to,” لشکر + گه (لشکرگاه) = “army‑camp” (gah marks place/occasion), خویش (خوی + ش) = “his own” (possessive), بنهاد (بنهادن) = “placed/put/turned” (past), and روی = “face.” The phrase therefore means “he turned his face toward his own army‑camp.”
segments.s151-g88: پراندیشه is an epithet meaning “full of thought / pensive.” جان و سرش = “his life and his head” (جان = life/soul, سر = head) with ش as the 3rd‑person possessive. کینه‌جوی is a compound: کینه = “hatred / grudge” and جوی (from جستن) = “seeker,” so together “seeking vengeance” or “vengeful.” Grammatically this tags the subject’s resolve: thoughtful, he sought vengeance with all his life and head.
segments.s151-g57: The phrase begins with the prepositional phrase به بالا (to/above) followed by the noun ستاره (star) as subject; بساید is a 3rd-person singular classical present/jussive from بسای(دن) meaning "to favor/benefit" and here carries an optative/benedictive sense "may (it) favor". The enclitic همی is a classical persistent/aspectual particle adding continuous/emphatic flavor ("ever/indeed"). Word order (prep + subject + verb + همی) is a poetic inversion common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s151-g58: تنش combines تن (body) + the 3rd-person possessive suffix ش ("his"), and را is the definite/object marker marking تنش as the object. زمین is the grammatical subject and برگراید is a present/optative 3rd-person verb (from برگردادن/برگردیدن) meaning "incline/turn toward"; همی again adds continuous/emphatic nuance. The clause thus reads literally "the earth may incline toward his body."
segments.s151-g59: دو بازو = "two arms" (numeral + noun). رانش here is the noun ران (thigh) plus the 3rd-person suffix -ش ("his thigh"); the poet writes ز + ران هیون ("from/of the thigh of the beast") to make a comparison or source: "his two arms and his thigh (compare) to the thigh of a beast." Note the verb/predicate is omitted here and picked up in the next segment, a common poetic device.
segments.s151-g60: همانا introduces an emphatic/attesting clause ("verily/indeed"); که is the relative/complementizer "that/which". دارد is the present tense "has" (3rd-person singular). ستبری فزون is a nominal phrase with ستبری (stoutness/thickness) followed by the adjective فزون (excessive/more than usual) — adjective after noun is normal in poetry. The clause supplies the predicate omitted before: "indeed (they) have great stoutness."
segments.s151-g61: This is an enumerative instrumental series introduced by repeated به (here used instrumentally: "with/by"): به گرز و به تیغ و به تیر و کمند = "with mace and with sword and with arrow and lasso." The repeated preposition is a poetic device emphasizing each instrument.
segments.s151-g62: ز هرگونه‌ای is a prepositional phrase meaning "of/from every kind" (here: "of every sort"); آزمودیم is the simple past first‑person plural "we tried/tested"; بند is the direct object "fetters/bonds." The order places the prepositional phrase first, verb next, and object at the end (poetic word order), so the clause reads "from every kind we tried bonds/fetters."
segments.s151-g63: سرانجام is an adverb "finally/at last." گفتم is the past‑tense 1st‑person singular "I said." که introduces the reported content clause. من is the explicit subject "I" and پیش ازین (پیش + از + این) is a temporal phrase "before this/prior to now." The clause frames the speaker's reported statement: "At last I said that I, before now,..."
segments.s151-g64: بسی here means "many/much" (frequent/intensive), گرد = "troop/band" (poetic sense), and را is the object marker attaching to بسی گرد. برگرفتم is the past tense 1st‑person singular "I took up/removed" — here idiomatically "I unseated" or "I have lifted off the saddle." ز زین is the prepositional phrase "from the saddle." Together: "I had often unseated many a band from the saddle."
segments.s151-g9: 'به هومان' is a prepositional phrase 'to Hūmān' (preposition + name). 'چنین گفت' is the reporting verb 'said thus' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past tense 'said') and introduces the following quoted clause; word order here is classical Persian: indirect object + manner + verb. 'کامروز هور' begins the reported content — 'کامروز' is a temporal adverb 'today', and 'هور' here is the subject of the upcoming verb in the next segment.
segments.s151-g10: This is the reported clause whose subject (هور) from the previous segment is understood: 'برآمد' is a past tense verb 'arose/appeared'. 'جهان کرد' is a causative construction where 'کرد' ('made') takes 'جهان' ('the world') as its object; classical word order places the object before the verb. 'پر' is a preposed modifier meaning 'full (of)', followed by the nouns 'چنگ و شور' ('harp and passion/tumult') that describe what the world was filled with.
segments.s151-g11: 'شما را' is the object phrase ('to you' or 'you' with the object marker را after شما). 'چه کرد' is the interrogative + past verb 'what did (he) do'. The subject 'آن سوار دلیر' ('that brave horseman') follows the verb, a common classical Persian order (object/adjuncts — verb — subject).
segments.s151-g12: The relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/that') modifies 'سوار'. 'یال یلان داشت' literally 'he had the mane of the yelān (heroes)', where 'یال' (mane) is in a genitive relation to 'یلان' (heroes); 'داشت' is past 'had'. 'و' links the second attribute 'آهنگ شیر' — here 'آهنگ' functions as 'bearing/pace/manner' (not 'melody'), so 'آهنگ شیر' = 'the bearing of a lion'.
segments.s151-g13: 'بدو گفت هومان' shows an inversion common in classical Persian: the indirect object 'بدو' ('to him') appears before the verb 'گفت' ('said'), and the subject 'هومان' follows the verb — literally 'to him said Hūmān'. 'که فرمان شاه' begins a subordinate content clause ('that the king's command ...').
segments.s151-g14: 'چنان ... کز' is a correlative/result construction meaning 'so/such ... that'. Here 'چنان' = 'so/thus' and 'بد' functions as a predicative adjective/adverb (classical usage: 'of such a sort'), followed by 'کز' = 'که از' ('that from/of which'). 'ایدر' = 'here', 'نجنبد' is a negative present/potential form 'does not/will not move/stir', and 'سپاه' ('the army') is the logical subject placed at the end. Altogether: 'so (commanding) that the army will not stir from here.'
segments.s151-g15: 'همه کار' = 'all/the whole affair(s)'. 'ماسخت' is an older/poetic adjective here meaning something like 'very hard' (a compound or classical form; the provided lemma senses do not match this poetic usage). 'ناساز' = 'unsuitable/unworkable', and 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The phrase states a general condition: all matters were harsh and ill-suited.
segments.s151-g16: 'به آورد گشتن' is a periphrastic infinitival expression meaning 'to bring about / to be brought about' (آورد here 'cause/bring about' with گشتن 'to become/arise' forming the verbal idea). 'چه' is exclamatory/interrogative 'what (a)', 'آغاز' = 'beginning', and 'بود' is the copula 'was'. The whole clause is an exclamatory remark on how difficult the initial effort to effect the thing was.
segments.s151-g73: بکوشم is a 1st‑person jussive/subjunctive with the b‑prefix (بـ) + stem کوش‑ + ending ‑م: 'I will/let me strive'. ندانم is the negative present 'I do not know' (ن + دانم). که introduces the embedded interrogative clause ('who...'), with پیروز (adjective/noun 'victorious') followed by the interrogative/relative کی 'who', and the copula ست 'is' at the end — typical poetic word order where the predicate (پیروز) precedes the interrogative.
segments.s151-g74: ببینیم is 1st‑person plural jussive/subjunctive 'let us see'. تا here introduces purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order to find out') and governs the clause رای یزدان به چیست 'what the counsel/plan of God concerning it is'. Note the ezāfe linking رای and یزدان is omitted in this poetic line; به functions as the preposition meaning 'about/in regard to', and چیست is the interrogative copula 'what is'.
segments.s151-g75: کزویست is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning 'that (it) is from him' (کز + وی + ست). The sequence پیروزی و فر و زور lists three nouns — 'victory', 'farr' (the special royal/divine glory), and 'might/strength' — as things that originate from him.
segments.s151-g76: هم او places emphasis ('he himself, too') before the predicate آفریننده 'creator' — the copula is omitted as is normal in Persian nominal sentences. ماه و هور names the created bodies; هور in classical/poetic Persian carries the sense of bright light or the sun.
segments.s151-g77: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (i.e., 'said to him') placed before گفت 'said' (past tense verb). کاووس is the speaker's name. یزدان پاک follows as a vocative/address ('O pure God' or 'pure God') introducing the invocation that follows.
segments.s151-g78: دل بدسگالت is the object ('your ill‑fated/ill‑omened heart'). کند is 3rd‑person present/subjunctive used optatively here ('may he do' / 'may he make'), so the whole clause is a wish/prayer. چاک چاک is a reduplicated onomatopoetic phrase meaning 'torn/rent to pieces' — repetition intensifies the action.
segments.s151-g79: من امشب is the topicalized subject/time 'I, tonight'. به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of' or 'before' (someone), and جهان‑آفرین is a compound noun 'world‑creator' (the Creator) — this entire phrase sets the place/person before whom the action will take place.
segments.s151-g80: بمالم is 1st‑person jussive/subjunctive of مالیدن 'to rub/press' (بـ + مال + ‑م) — 'I will press/lay'. فراوان is an adverb 'many times/abundantly'. دو رخ literally 'two faces' here means 'both cheeks'. بر زمین is the prepositional phrase 'upon the ground' completing the action.
segments.s151-g89: This is a compact clause: the proper name زواره is the subject, بیامد is a simple past verb 'came', and خلیده روان is a post-verbal descriptive phrase ('of secluded/tranquil spirit') that predicates a quality of the subject. Classical Persian often places such adjectival or nominal descriptions after the verb without an explicit copula.
segments.s151-g90: که introduces a subordinate content clause ('to find out/that'), with the interrogative/adverbial چون 'how' followed by the past copula بود 'was'; امروز is the time adverb and بر پهلوان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with/regarding the champion' (بر = 'against/regarding'). Word order is typical classical Persian: question-word + copula + adverb + prepositional phrase.
segments.s151-g91: The clause places the oblique phrase ازو 'from him' before the object خوردنی 'something to eat' and the past verb خواست 'asked/wanted'; the subject رستم follows the verb (common in older poetic word order). نخست 'first' is an adverb modifying the action. So literally: 'From him food asked Rostam first.'
segments.s151-g92: پس آنگه is an emphatic pair meaning 'afterwards/then'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from', taking اندیشگان as the source ('from thoughts/the thoughtful') and دل as the object being acted on. بشست is the past of شستن 'he washed/cleansed', so the whole means 'then he cleansed his heart from (troubling) thoughts' — an idiomatic way of saying he cast off anxieties.
segments.s151-g93: چنین ('thus') modifies the past verb راند ('sent forth/uttered'). پیش برادر is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of his brother' and سخن is the object 'words/speech' — word order is verb-centered and somewhat inverted: 'Thus he sent forth words before his brother.'
segments.s151-g94: که introduces the reported imperative content. بیدار دل باش is an imperative clause built from the adjective بیدار 'alert' + noun دل 'heart' followed by the imperative باش 'be' ('be of alert heart'). و coordinates the following negative imperative تندی مکن — تندی here means 'haste' and مکن is the negative imperative 'do not do'.
segments.s151-g95: This is an adverbial phrase of manner and destination: به شبگیر 'as a night-raider' uses به to form a manner/role phrase, چُـون من 'like me' is a comparative, and به آوردگاه is the destination 'to the battlefield'. Note the duplicated به: the first forms the manner phrase, the second marks direction.
segments.s151-g96: روم here is the poetic 1st-person verb 'I go/will go' (classical/subjunctive/present form used in poetry). پیش آن ترک آوردخواه is a prepositional phrase 'before that Turk (آند ترُک) who is آوردخواه' — آوردخواه is a compound meaning 'one who desires to bring/engage' (i.e. one eager to bring on/engage in battle). The verb is followed by its situational prepositional phrase.
segments.s151-g113: This is a simple clause with the indefinite/topic word 'کس' ('no one') fronted, followed by the locative phrase 'اندر جهان' ('in the world'), and the predicate 'جاودانه نماند'. 'جاودانه' is an adjective 'eternal/everlasting' and 'نماند' is the past (3rd sg.) of ماندن with negation 'ن-' meaning 'did not remain/last'. Together they express 'did not remain eternal' (i.e., no one is eternal).
segments.s151-g114: 'ز گردون' is a prepositional phrase ('from/by the heavens' — here implying fate/heaven). 'مرا' is the accusative clitic sequence (written here as 'م' + 'را') meaning 'to me/for me' (object). 'خود' adds emphasis 'for myself', 'بهانه' is the noun 'excuse/pretext', and 'نماند' again is the past negative of ماندن 'there remained not'. The whole segment says that, by the heavens/fate, there remained no excuse for the speaker.
segments.s151-g115: 'بسی' is a modifier meaning 'many' (classical/poetic), followed by a coordinated list of nouns joined by و ('and'): 'شیر' (lion), 'دیو' (demon/monster), 'پلنگ' (leopard), 'نهنگ' (whale/huge beast). This whole noun phrase is the object/theme of the following verb phrase.
segments.s151-g116: The predicate 'تبه شد' uses تبه (here 'ruin/being ruined') + شد (past 3rd sg. 'became/was'), yielding 'were ruined' or 'were put to ruin'. 'به چنگم' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'into my grasp' (به + چنگ with the possessive -م), where چنگم denotes 'my grip/hand/hold'. 'به هنگام جنگ' is a time phrase 'at the time of battle' (the fixed expression 'به هنگام' + noun). The clause thus states these creatures were overcome and ended up in the speaker's grasp in battle.
segments.s151-g117: 'بسی باره' means 'many times/occasions' (classical use of بسی + باره), followed by 'و دژ' 'and fortresses/citadels'. 'که کردیم پست' is a relative clause: 'که' (that/who), 'کردیم' (we made/did — past 1st pl.), and 'پست' (low/brought low). The construction 'کردیم پست' = 'we made (them) low' i.e. 'we humbled/razed (them)'.
segments.s151-g118: This line is inverted for poetic effect. 'نیاورد' is the past of آوردن with negation 'did not bring/put'. 'کس' is the (indefinite) subject 'no one'. 'دست من' literally 'my hand' (دست + possessive من). 'زیر دست' is an idiom meaning 'under the hand' → 'under someone's control/subordinate'. Put together (with poetic word order): 'No one put me under (another's) hand' — i.e. 'no one made me subordinate.'
segments.s151-g119: 'در مرگ را' is 'the door of death' (در = door/gate; مرگ = death) with را marking the definite/direct object. 'آن' is the demonstrative 'that one/he', and 'بکوبد' is the present/subjunctive/epic present of کوفتن/کوبیدن meaning 'strikes' or 'knocks'. 'که پای' begins a relative clause 'whose foot...' that continues in the next segment. The construction reads proverbially: 'Death's door is struck by that one who...' .
segments.s151-g120: 'باسپ' here is an archaic/poetic element immediately modifying پای in the previous segment (likely a fused/old form meaning something like 'of the horse' or 'mounted/horse‑related'); its exact modern sense is not offered in the provided senses. 'اندر آرد' is 'puts/ brings in' (آرد, 3rd sg. 'brings/puts' with اندر = 'into/in'), and 'بجنبد' is 3rd sg. present of جنبیدن 'moves/stirs'. 'ز جای' is 'from its place'. Grammatically this completes the relative clause: 'whose (horse-)foot he puts/whose foot slips and then moves from its place.'
segments.s151-g49: غمی گشت is a predicative phrase in which غمی (a nominal 'a grief/sorrow') precedes the past verb گشت (from گشتن, 'became/turned into') — a common classical inversion meaning 'became sorrowful'. ز گفتار اوی is a prepositional/causal phrase 'from/by his speech'; اوی is an archaic third‑person possessive ('his').
segments.s151-g50: بر شاه کاووس بنهاد روی literally puts the preposition بر + object (شاه کاووس) before the verb بنهاد ('placed/put/turned') and the noun روی ('face') after it; the construction means 'he turned/laid (his) face toward King Kavus'. Note the classical word order where the verb follows the prepositional phrase and the possessed noun روی is the thing 'placed'.
segments.s151-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. The clause has Kaavus as subject (کاووس) and پهلوان را (with را marking the definite object 'the champion') as object; بدید is the past tense 'saw'. The word کی here functions as an archaic relative/particle (similar to که/که‌) linking the clause.
segments.s151-g52: بر خویش نزدیک جایش گزید is idiomatic: بر خویش (بِ + خوی + ش) means 'toward himself/his side' with the enclitic ش marking 'his'; نزدیک modifies جای ('place/seat'); گزید (past of گزیدن) here means 'took/chose' — so 'he took his place near him'. Note the enclitic ش attaching to both خوی and جای to show possession.
segments.s151-g53: ز سهراب رستم زبان برگشاد is a classical order: ز سهراب ('concerning Sohrab') is a topical/prepositional phrase, رستم is the subject, and زبان برگشاد ('opened his tongue') is an idiom meaning 'he began to speak' (برگشاد is the past participle/verb 'opened').
segments.s151-g54: ز بالا و برزش همی کرد یاد: ز introduces the content remembered ('of his loftiness and his greatness'); بالا is 'loftiness/high rank', برزش is 'his greatness/valor' (بر + -ش = his merit), همی is the habitual/continuous particle used in classical Persian ('was/kept on'), کرد is the past 'did', and یاد is the noun 'remembrance' — together 'he remembered/recalled his high rank and greatness.'
segments.s151-g55: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('that'); کس در جهان is 'anyone/any person in the world' (کس + locative در جهان); کودک نارسید uses نارسید in an archaic sense of 'did not come into being/was not born/was not matched' — the clause therefore expresses the idea 'that no one in the world came forth/appeared ...'.
segments.s151-g56: بدین شیرمردی و گردی ندید: بدین is the classical contraction of ب + این ('with/to this'); شیرمردی = 'manliness/heroism' and گردی here is used poetically for a kindred idea of prowess; ندید is the negated past 'was not seen' — the idiom means 'one was not seen possessing such manliness and prowess.' The clause thus completes the relative: 'that no one in the world was seen with such heroism and prowess.'
segments.s151-g17: بیامی is an archaic/perhaps poetic perfect/past form meaning 'came' (3rd person). یکی is an indefinite determiner 'one/a certain' placed before the noun. مرد پرخاشجوی is a noun + adjective phrase ('man' + 'quarrelsome/aggressive'), with the adjective post‑nominal as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s151-g18: برین لشکر is a compressed form of بر این لشکر = 'against/at this army' (بر = 'against/on', ین = orthographic variant of 'این' = 'this'); گشن is an adjective modifying لشکر ('hungry' or 'famished' as a descriptive epithet of the host). بنهاد is an archaic past verb 'he set/put' used here with روی ('face') to form بنهاد روی = 'he turned/placed his face (toward)'; روی is the noun 'face' and is the object of بنهاد.
segments.s151-g19: تو گفتی is 'you said' (2nd person past) introducing reported speech. ز مستی is a causal/ablative phrase 'from/because of drunkenness' (ز = 'from/because of', مستی = 'drunkenness'). کنون is the adverb 'now/just now' placed before the verb. خاستست is an archaic 3rd singular past (خاست + -ست) meaning 'he rose/stood up'.
segments.s151-g20: وگر is a conditional/concessive 'but if' or 'and if'. جنگ is the noun 'war/battle' (object of the verb in the clause). بایک تن is a fused prepositional phrase = با یک تن ('with a single person') where بایک is compressed. آراستست is an archaic perfect/resultado form (آراستن + -ست) meaning 'he has armed/arrayed himself' or 'is prepared/arranged' for battle; verb comes at the end in this poetic word order.
segments.s151-g21: چنین گفت سهراب is inversional word order common in poetry: 'Thus spoke Sohrab' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت used here as the verb 'said', سهراب the subject). کاو is a linking/relative particle (classical use ≈ 'that/which/whom' or 'who'), introducing the subordinate clause. زین سپاه is 'from/among this army' (ز/از + این combined), indicating the domain being spoken about.
segments.s151-g22: نکرد is the negative past of کردن = 'did not do/caused' and here takes the predicate that follows. از introduces a partitive/agent sense 'from/of'. دلیران is the plural adjective/noun 'the brave' (used as a noun). کسی را after a negated verb means 'anyone' (کسی here = 'anyone'), with را as the definite/object marker. تباه is the adjective/participle 'ruined/destroyed' forming the predicate 'did not destroy anyone'.
segments.s151-g23: از ایرانیان is a partitive phrase 'of/among the Iranians' (از = 'from/of'). من is the 1st person pronoun 'I' (subject). بسی is an old/poetic quantifier 'many/much' (here 'many'). کشته‌ام is a perfect/result form meaning 'I have killed' (past participle + auxiliary), so the clause means 'I have killed many among the Iranians.'
segments.s151-g24: زمین را is the direct object 'the earth' with the object marker را. به خون و گل uses به with a comitative/instrumental sense 'with/by' to list means/substances: 'with blood and clay/mud' (گل here poetically as 'mud' or 'clotted earth'). آغشته‌ام is a participial adjective + 1st singular auxiliary meaning 'I have made (it) wet/stained' or 'it is soaked by me' — together: 'I have soaked the earth with blood and mud.'
segments.s151-g65: گرفتم is first-person past 'I took/seized'. دوال ('twofold, both') modifies کمربند ('belt/girth') and precedes it for emphasis — a common poetic ordering. اوی is a possessive/third-person marker 'his', attached after the noun phrase (poetic word order: verb — numeral/adjective — noun — possessive).
segments.s151-g66: بیفشاردم is a first-person past-form indicating an action of pressing/tightening (here 'I drew/tightened'); in classical poetry the prefix بـ can appear fused to the verb. سخت is an adverb 'firmly/strongly' modifying پیوند ('fastening, bond, strap'). اوی again is the possessive 'his', linking the whole phrase to the same person as before.
segments.s151-g67: همی خواستم combines the particle همی (a classical/progressive/intent marker) with خواستم 'I wanted/attempted', giving 'I intended/was about to'. کش is the action 'to pull' placed before the prepositional phrase ز زین (poetic ordering); برکنم is a present-subjunctive/infinitival form 'to remove/pull off' used after خواستم to express the intended action ('to pull off from the saddle').
segments.s151-g68: چو here introduces a comparative/temporal clause 'as/so that, like'; دیگر کسانش means 'his other people/others' (i.e., 'like the others of his'), with کسانش a pronominalized plural 'his people/companions'. به خاک is the direction/result 'to the ground'; افگنم is an old subjunctive/present-form of افکندن 'to cast/throw', used here for the intended result ('that I might throw him').
segments.s151-g69: گر is the conditional 'if'. از باد جنبان شود is a passive/causative-style clause 'be shaken/moved by the wind' (جنبان 'shaking', شود 'becomes/shall be'), with the agent 'باد' following the preposition از. کوه خار — literally 'mountain (of) thorn(s)' — is the subject phrase placed after the verb for poetic inversion ('a mountain of thorns' that is shaken).
segments.s151-g70: نجنبید is a negative jussive/optative-like form meaning 'would not stir/move' (the initial ن negates جنبیدن). The doubled بر (بر زین بر) stacks prepositional phrases: بر زین = 'on the saddle' and the second بر links that phrase to آن نامدار 'that renowned one' (i.e. 'on the saddle of that famed man'). The word order (verb then prepositional phrases then modifier) is characteristic of epic poetry.
segments.s151-g71: چو فردا بیاید is a temporal conditional 'when/tomorrow he comes' where بیاید is a present-subjunctive ('may come' / 'comes') used in a time clause. به دشت نبرد literally 'to the plain of battle' — دشت modified by نبرد — means 'to the battlefield/plain to fight', with the preposition به marking movement toward that place.
segments.s151-g72: به کشتی is an instrumental phrase 'by/with wrestling' (به used instrumentally). همی بایدم is 'I must' in archaic/epic contraction: همی (progressive/emphatic) + بایدم (باید + -م for first person). چاره کرد is an infinitival phrase 'to make/find a remedy/solution' (چاره 'remedy/means' + کردن 'to do'), here governed by باید to mean 'I must find a way.'

sentences.s151-s1: They departed, and the face of the sky grew dark; because of Sohrab the heavens stood staring in astonishment.
sentences.s151-s2: You said that the sky was produced/created from his fighting; it could not be pacified for a single moment because of his charging.
sentences.s151-s3: And if at any time beneath his inward part there is iron, his soul is wondrous and his body is iron-clad.
sentences.s151-s4: A dark night came toward his army, and in the midst (thereof) — from the fighting and from his dagger.
sentences.s151-s61: Even if you live more than a thousand years, this will be the outcome of the matter.

sentences.s151-s64: We are all subject to death, old and young; in the world no one remains eternal.
